Selected work

Case studies

Case study 01

Message Capping NUX

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped March 2026

$1B+ product line

Content leadership Cross-functional Monetization NUX

Problem

WhatsApp was introducing chat cap limits, and four teams disagreed on how to frame it. But the real problem wasn't the copy. It was the framing. Each team had a different answer for what this moment was: a product launch, a policy enforcement, a consumer protection measure, or a business transparency signal. The copy reflected that confusion.

What I did

Before suggesting any copy, I made a strategic call: establish what this moment actually was. I argued internally that we weren't announcing a feature. We were setting a standard. That shift changed everything. From there, I paired every recommendation with a written rationale: "overload" over "unwanted" because overload is about volume, not judgment. "Haven't responded to you" over "never messaged you" because it's more accurate and less punitive. When GTM pushed back, worried that naming the cap directly might create consumer backlash, I proposed a third path: an informational-only half-sheet with a toast notification. No editorial framing, no value prop, just the facts. GTM and Product accepted, resolving 53 review comments without escalation.

Outcome

My informational positioning was adopted as GTM's standard going forward. Capping contributed to Business Messaging Monetization's 170% incremental revenue increase and 7x monthly revenue lift.

+170%

Case study 02

Dynamic Audiences Naming

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped April 2026

$1B+ product line

Naming systems Information architecture 74 countries

Problem

SMB users navigated jargon like "Set as dynamic audience," "List name," and table-formatted descriptions just to reach their own customers. The existing labels had been inherited from database-side architecture, and a naming standard didn't exist.

What I did

I pushed for a naming standard where none existed. My process started with the user journey, not the database schema showing up verbatim in the UI. I mapped suggested audience names with logical descriptions, asking what each audience represented and how a name reinforced it. I landed on "Active chats," "Inactive chats," and "Your WhatsApp contacts" to name collections a small business owner could recognize. I also flagged that the active/inactive split was confusing because both lists used a 30-day cutoff, meaning the same chat could appear in both. Rather than treating this as a copy problem, I escalated it as a product logic bug for engineering to fix. Research validated the label framework with real users before we finalized.

Outcome

Naming became the standard across all audience surfaces, shipped in 74 countries. Users were getting to their goal faster because the language made sense to them.

4.3×
Picker-to-Composer conversion
1.4×
Composer-to-Draft conversion
−28.6%
Contact picker time (M1)

Naming exploration: before & variants

Case study 03

GenAI Content Strategy

Meta · WhatsApp Business · Shipped May 2026

GenAI Image Composer

GenAI Content strategy Terminology NUX

Problem

WhatsApp Business was integrating GenAI, and technical vocabulary had been baked into the entire surface from the start. The word "generate" implies the AI does all the work and always delivers something perfect. The first time a user gets an undesirable result, they feel like the product failed. That's a trust problem, not just a word problem.

What I did

I started with a question: what expectation does "generate" set, and does the product actually meet it? "Create" puts the user in the driver's seat, implying collaboration and iteration, not a takeover. I mapped the full content journey so every label, CTA, and screen name felt like part of a coherent pattern. I named the screen "Create AI images" and resolved ambiguous CTAs, moving from "Select how you want to generate: From a message or From a photo" to "Start by using a message, photo, or description: Use a message or Use a photo."

Outcome

The "create" terminology was adopted as the standard across GenAI surfaces. The content strategy question here is the same one I've always worked on: how do you help users understand what a product can and can't do, so they trust it enough to keep using it?

+22.8%
Messages delivered
+26.1%
Messages read
+25.6%
Messages replied

Education & NUX: before, preferred, and shipped

Charles Schwab

Advisor-client trading UI

Designed dual-view content for margin and options trading: advisors see full strategy details, clients see only what's been selected, reducing trading errors and improving trust.

Charles Schwab

Error message rewrite

Simplified an overly apologetic, multi-line 404 into two words and one sentence. Shifted ownership: "Can't find page" (our issue) became "Page not found" (accurate framing).

Reduced cognitive load

Google

String review at scale

Set a goal of 750 strings reviewed in 3 months. Built a scalable TextID-based workflow and reviewed 3,500+ strings, 4× the target, without changing underlying code.

3,500+ strings · scalable process

Content Design & Strategy roles

Over 15 years of experience in UX writing, content strategy, and information architecture across enterprise, consumer, and fintech products.

I'm a Senior Content Designer and UX Writer with a career spanning Meta, Apple, Google, Schwab, and beyond. I specialize in high-stakes, regulated, and monetization-sensitive environments where the words have to earn their place.

My work lives at the intersection of clarity, systems thinking, and user trust. I believe the best content decisions come from understanding the whole product, not just the string.

When I'm not writing, I'm at Stinson Beach, walking my beagle Princess Leia, or sharpening my storytelling at Toastmasters.

Let's work together.
